risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Subjects were then randomised 1:1 using a computer-generated randomisation list using randomisation blocks of four. These blocks were assigned to study centres." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation method (computer-generated randomisation list with blocked randomisation), which is sufficient for Low risk of bias in sequence generation.

_evidence.method_quote: Subjects were then randomised 1:1 using a computer-generated randomisation list using randomisation blocks of four. These blocks were assigned to study centres.
_evidence.method_summary: Participants were randomized 1:1 with a computer-generated list in blocks of four assigned to centers.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Within each centre the allocation of a randomisation number was linked in a chronologically ascending manner to the sequence of arrival of the patients at the study centre." Comment: This indicates a predictable enrolment-linked assignment process, so the recruiter could foresee the upcoming allocation for the next arriving participant. No explicit concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation or SNOSE) is described.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Subjects were then randomised 1:1 using a computer-generated randomisation list using randomisation blocks of four. These blocks were assigned to study centres. Within each centre the allocation of a randomisation number was linked in a chronologically ascending manner to the sequence of arrival of the patients at the study centre.
predictability_signals: chronologically ascending manner to the sequence of arrival of the patients at the study centre
_evidence.note: The article describes sequence generation and centre/block assignment, but it does not describe any method of allocation concealment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a Phase III, multicentre, open-label, randomised, active-controlled, parallel-group study" Comment: The study explicitly states it was open-label, so participants and personnel were not blinded. Many of the main outcomes were subjective or patient-reported, including "days with dysmenorrhoeic pain," "days with pelvic pain," "days with dysmenorrhoeic pain that interfered with daily activities," "investigator-assessed global improvement in dysmenorrhoea," and "user satisfaction," so knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect the outcomes. Because blinding was absent and the outcomes could be influenced, this domain is high risk.

_evidence.blinding_quote: This was a Phase III, multicentre, open-label, randomised, active-controlled, parallel-group study
outcome_categories: days with at least moderate dysmenorrhoeic pain
days with dysmenorrhoeic pain associated with withdrawal or unscheduled bleeding
days with dysmenorrhoeic pain that interfered with daily activities
subjective_outcomes: days with at least moderate dysmenorrhoeic pain
days with dysmenorrhoeic pain associated with withdrawal or unscheduled bleeding
days with dysmenorrhoeic pain that interfered with daily activities

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Pain severity was self-assessed on a daily basis using a verbal rating scale" and "investigator-assessed global improvement in dysmenorrhoea (seven-point scale...)"; the study was "open-label". Comment: The outcome assessment was performed by patients for the main pain outcomes and by investigators for a clinician-rated global improvement scale, and the trial was explicitly open-label. Because at least one key outcome is subjective/judgement-dependent and no blinding of outcome assessment is described, knowledge of allocation could bias measurement. Under the worst-outcome rule, the subjective and patient-reported outcomes make the overall domain high risk.

outcomes.label: number of days with dysmenorrhoeic pain
days with at least moderate dysmenorrhoeic pain
days with dysmenorrhoeic pain associated with withdrawal or unscheduled bleeding
days with dysmenorrhoeic pain that interfered with daily activities

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A total of 223 patients who received study medication were included in the FAS (flexibleMIB regimen, n=115; conventional regimen, n=108) and 210 patients (110 and 100, respectively) completed the study as planned." Comment: The reported post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcome was low (223 in the full analysis set vs 210 completers; total loss about 5.8%) and balanced across arms (115 vs 110; 108 vs 100), with no evidence of outcome-related exclusions or substantial differential missingness. The article also states that "All women who took at least one unit of study medication and for whom at least one observation after dosing was available were included in the full analysis set (FAS)," which supports an appropriate analysis set for the primary efficacy outcome.

_evidence.notes: Primary efficacy variable analysed in the full analysis set; article reports 223 in FAS and 210 completed as planned, but does not provide per-arm dropout reasons or randomised denominators in the excerpted text.
